- test values / measurements, not just “inspected”

Red flags I’d treat as cleanup language:
- “as needed” when the equipment should have a fixed interval
- “per manufacturer recommendations” with no attached schedule
- “service completed” with no technician name or timestamp
- same wording repeated every month like a copy-paste job
- no record of skipped intervals, ever. That’s usually fantasy
- work orders closed but no readings, no parts, no findings

If the contract is trying to gloss over missed work, the weak point is usually the gap between the promised interval and the actual completion record. That’s where I’d press. If you paste the schedule language, I can mark the exact holes in it.

Yeah, direction’s fine, but it’s still pretty broad.

I’d want it tighter — like what kind of equipment, what symptom, and what numbers actually matter. Otherwise it’s just "watch it more," which isn’t much help.

For me the useful split is:
- **one-off anomaly with no repeatable condition** = noise
- **same symptom under same load/temp/startup/state** = investigate
- **any drift in a measured parameter** = don’t hand-wave it

And I’d log it like a failure report, not a diary. Time, state, reading, what changed, what didn’t.

If you want this to be useful, give me the equipment type and the exact off behavior. Then I can tell you what to track and what thresholds would actually matter.

- timestamp / date / revision so nobody backfills the record later

retrieved_memories.content:
Yeah, that’s the right shape. Factual, chronological, narrow. No drama.

What I’d verify before sending:
- exact date and time, not just “around” unless that’s all you actually know
- names, titles, and who actually saw it
- what you personally observed vs what somebody else said
- any quote you want to use, word for word if possible
- photos, emails, logs, ticket numbers, repair notes, whatever exists
- the order things happened in
- whether you’re implying a policy or safety issue you can actually back up

And in the first draft, I’d keep the complaint to:
- who
- what
- when
- where
- immediate effect
- who you notified
- what proof you have
- what you want done

No conclusions unless you can defend them. That part matters. I’ve seen people get sloppy there and hand the other side an opening.

If you want, I can help you turn rough notes into something tight and defensible.

| Item | Option A | Option B | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|

| Can inspect/test after |  |  |  |
| Risk if wrong |  |  |  |
| Undo / rework effort |  |  |  |
| Hidden problem potential |  |  |  |
| Evidence supporting it |  |  |  |

Then force a call on the last column. That’s the part people skip.

If you want the main difference obvious, use one line at the top:

**A is safer if ___ ; B is better if ___.**

That usually cuts through the noise fast.

retrieved_memories.content:
Yeah, that’s the right shape. Factual, chronological, narrow. No drama.

What I’d verify before sending:
- exact date and time, not just “around” unless that’s all you actually know
- names, titles, and who actually saw it
- what you personally observed vs what somebody else said
- any quote you want to use, word for word if possible
- photos, emails, logs, ticket numbers, repair notes, whatever exists
- the order things happened in
- whether you’re implying a policy or safety issue you can actually back up

And in the first draft, I’d keep the complaint to:
- who
- what
- when
- where
- immediate effect
- who you notified
- what proof you have
- what you want done

No conclusions unless you can defend them. That part matters. I’ve seen people get sloppy there and hand the other side an opening.

If you want, I can help you turn rough notes into something tight and defensible.

Close, but still a little thin. I’d want the sheet to force an actual call, not just list factors.

retrieved_memories.content:
Yeah, that’s the right shape. But I’d want it tighter. Too much free-text and it turns into noise fast.

What I’d actually use on the phone:
- one note per issue, or one sheet with one row per event
- same fields every time
- timestamps auto-filled if possible
- photos named the same way as the note entry

Minimum fields I’d keep:
- date/time
- location
- exact symptom
- conditions right before it
- evidence: photo, video, reading, log, whatever
- what changed after your action
- whether it repeated

And for comparison later, use categories, not just descriptions. Like:
- severity: low / medium / high
- repeat rate: first time / recurring / constant
- state: on / off / hot / cold / loaded / idle

That makes patterns obvious without rereading a bunch of prose.

If you want, give me the kind of issue it is — equipment, building, car, phone, whatever — and I’ll strip this into a cleaner template that fits that case.
